root_text.sn17.37:1.2: “Dāruṇo, bhikkhave, lābhasakkārasiloko kaṭuko pharuso antarāyiko anuttarassa yogakkhemassa adhigamāya.
root_text.sn17.37:1.3: Idhāhaṁ, bhikkhave, ekaccaṁ puggalaṁ evaṁ cetasā ceto paricca pajānāmi:
root_text.sn17.37:1.5: Tamenaṁ passāmi aparena samayena lābhasakkārasilokena abhibhūtaṁ pariyādiṇṇacittaṁ sampajānamusā bhāsantaṁ.
root_text.sn17.37:1.6: Evaṁ dāruṇo kho, bhikkhave, lābhasakkārasiloko kaṭuko pharuso antarāyiko anuttarassa yogakkhemassa adhigamāya.
root_text.sn17.37:1.9: Na ca no uppanno lābhasakkārasiloko cittaṁ pariyādāya ṭhassatī’ti.

translation_text.sn17.37:1.2: “Possessions, honor, and popularity are grim, bitter, and harsh. They’re an obstacle to reaching the supreme sanctuary from the yoke.
translation_text.sn17.37:1.3: When I’ve comprehended the mind of a certain individual, I understand:
translation_text.sn17.37:1.4: ‘This venerable would not tell a deliberate lie even for the sake of their mother.’
translation_text.sn17.37:1.5: But some time later I see them tell a deliberate lie because their mind is overcome and overwhelmed by possessions, honor, and popularity.
translation_text.sn17.37:1.6: So grim are possessions, honor, and popularity—bitter and harsh, an obstacle to reaching the supreme sanctuary from the yoke.
translation_text.sn17.37:1.7: So you should train like this:
translation_text.sn17.37:1.8: ‘We will give up arisen possessions, honor, and popularity, and we won’t let them occupy our minds.’
